metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Literary Studies 12 is a rigorous senior course focused on the close reading, analysis, and discussion of complex literary texts across genres and traditions. Students develop sophisticated interpretive skills, learn to situate texts within their social and historical contexts, and build the academic writing skills needed for post-secondary study. The course engages deeply with First Peoples texts and perspectives and examines how language constructs social and cultural identities.
metadata.general_description: A rigorous senior literary studies course focused on sophisticated textual analysis, comparative reading, and academic writing across diverse literary traditions including First Peoples literature.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Senior literary study engages with the most complex and challenging questions that literature poses about human experience, justice, and meaning.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their worldviews and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical literary theory makes explicit how different reading frameworks — feminist, post-colonial, psychoanalytic, Marxist — reveal different dimensions of a text's meaning.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 12, students situate texts within their full historical and cultural contexts, analyzing how those contexts are embedded in the text's language, form, and ideology.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Senior literary analysts attend to the specific work language performs — how words, syntax, and form construct identity, reinforce ideology, or resist dominant narratives.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: The critical reader developed through literary study is also a critical citizen — equipped to question power, resist manipulation, and think with nuance about complex issues.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and personal goals; understand and appreciate First Peoples perspectives, diversity, and land/place influence.
i_can_statements: I can read challenging literary texts for both pleasure and rigorous intellectual engagement.
I can analyze First Peoples texts with full understanding of their cultural, legal, and historical context.
I can analyze how land, place, and territory function in First Peoples literature.

competencies.raw: Use information for diverse purposes; evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability; select and apply appropriate strategies to comprehend, guide inquiry, and transform thinking.
i_can_statements: I can conduct literary research using scholarly databases, critical editions, and academic journals.
I can apply multiple reading strategies to transform my understanding of complex or resistant texts.
I can follow a rigorous literary inquiry from question to argument to evidence.

competencies.raw: Understand and appreciate how different forms and features reflect purposes; think critically and creatively to analyze ideas within, between, and beyond texts.
i_can_statements: I can conduct a comparative analysis of two or more literary texts, identifying thematic and formal connections.
I can analyze how a text's genre and form are inseparable from its ideological content.
I can synthesize ideas from multiple texts and sources into a complex, original argument.

competencies.raw: Recognize and analyze personal, social, cultural contexts; understand how language constructs identities; construct connections; analyze literary elements; recognize text structures; discern nuanced word meanings.
i_can_statements: I can apply concepts from literary theory (post-colonialism, feminism, New Historicism) to my analysis.
I can trace the nuanced semantic weight of specific words across a literary text.
I can analyze how a text's structure — its form of beginning, middle, and end — is itself meaningful.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding and transform thinking.
i_can_statements: I can engage in sophisticated literary dialogue, building genuinely on others' ideas.
I can represent and fairly test interpretations different from my own.
I can articulate complex literary ideas clearly in both conversation and writing.

competencies.raw: Respond to text personally, creatively, and critically; select and apply speaking and listening skills for varied formal and informal contexts; use writing and design processes.
i_can_statements: I can write literary analyses that are both intellectually rigorous and personally engaged.
I can present a literary argument persuasively in formal oral or written contexts.
I can produce diverse literary response forms: analysis, comparison, personal essay, creative response.

competencies.raw: Express and support opinions with evidence; reflect, assess, and refine texts; use conventions proficiently; use citations; transform ideas to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can write a sophisticated literary essay with a nuanced thesis, specific evidence, and analytical commentary.
I can revise my analytical writing to eliminate vagueness, improve argument structure, and increase precision.
I can produce original literary texts that demonstrate senior-level understanding of craft.

areas.name: Text Forms, Genres, Features and Structures
areas.raw: Features and structures of First Peoples texts; narrative structures found in First Peoples texts; protocols related to ownership.
topics: The literary canon and its critique: what gets included and why
World literature: texts from multiple cultural traditions
First Peoples literature: Canadian, global, oral and written
Genre theory: how genres evolve, hybridize, and carry ideological content
Narrative structures: linear, cyclical, iterative, fragmented, non-linear
Point of view: reliability, free indirect discourse, multiple focalization

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Multimodal writing strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes; reading strategies; oral language strategies.
topics: Close reading: sustained, evidence-based analysis of specific passages
Comparative reading: finding meaningful connections across texts
Literary theory: applying frameworks to illuminate texts
Research writing: integrating secondary sources effectively
Extended essay or culminating literary project

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; usage and conventions; citation techniques; literary elements and devices.
topics: Advanced analysis of diction: etymological, historical, and cultural dimensions of word choice
Syntactic analysis: how sentence structure creates rhetorical and aesthetic effects
Narrative technique: irony, unreliability, perspective, voice
Symbol, allegory, myth: how literature embeds larger meanings
Intertextuality and allusion at the senior level
Introduction to literary theory: post-colonialism, feminism, Marxism, reader-response, New Historicism
MLA citation and Works Cited for literary research
